reasoning:
The current observed metric for 'Violence against civilians' in French Polynesia is 0.083 per 30-day average over the last 360 days, which amounts to exactly 1 event over the entire year. This provides a baseline rate of 1 event per year. Applying a Poisson distribution (λ = 1/12), the probability of at least one event happening in a typical 30-day period is approximately 8%. Exhaustive searches found no news reports of any recent civilian violence or organized armed group activity in French Polynesia (unlike New Caledonia, which has seen extensive violence), suggesting the situation remains very peaceful. 

For the first resolution date (2025-06-29), the 30-day observation window started on May 30. Twenty-three of those 30 days have already elapsed without any documented incidents, leaving only 7 unobserved days, which lowers the probability to around 3-4%. 
For the next dates, the windows are completely in the future, so the baseline of 8% is used for the July 22 window. Following the instruction that uncertainty should increase with the forecast horizon and drive distant dates closer to 0.5, probabilities for the September and December windows are progressively adjusted upward to 11% and 15% to account for unforeseen long-term disturbances.
